texts.label: text
texts.orig: **Chicken tikka** is a chicken dish originating in the Punjab region of the [Indian Subcontinent](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Cuisine_of_India) made by baking [chicken](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Chicken) which has been [marinated](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Marinating) in [spices](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Spices_and_herbs) and [yoghurt](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Yogurt) . It should not be confused with [Chicken Tikka Masala](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Chicken_Tikka_Masala) (of which it is an ingredient). It is traditionally cooked on [skewers](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Skewer) in a [tandoor](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Tandoor) and is usually boneless. It is tasty when served with yellow rice and a spicy salad.
texts.text: **Chicken tikka** is a chicken dish originating in the Punjab region of the [Indian Subcontinent](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Cuisine_of_India) made by baking [chicken](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Chicken) which has been [marinated](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Marinating) in [spices](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Spices_and_herbs) and [yoghurt](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Yogurt) . It should not be confused with [Chicken Tikka Masala](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Chicken_Tikka_Masala) (of which it is an ingredient). It is traditionally cooked on [skewers](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Skewer) in a [tandoor](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Tandoor) and is usually boneless. It is tasty when served with yellow rice and a spicy salad.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1-4 chopped fresh red [chillis](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Chili_Pepper) (depending on strength of the chillis and the desired strength of the marinade)
texts.text: 1-4 chopped fresh red [chillis](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Chili_Pepper) (depending on strength of the chillis and the desired strength of the marinade)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Add all of the ingredients except the chicken to a non-metallic [mixing bowl](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Mixing_Bowl) , and mix well.
texts.text: Add all of the ingredients except the chicken to a non-metallic [mixing bowl](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Mixing_Bowl) , and mix well.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Add the chicken and mix until fully coated.
texts.text: Add the chicken and mix until fully coated.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Ideally, the chicken should now be left in a refrigerator to [marinate](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Marinating) for at least 24 hours. It may not be safe to leave chicken which has previously been frozen for longer than 24 hours, but fresh chicken should be left to marinate for 48 hours for best results. If you do not wish to leave the chicken to marinate, simply continue to the next step.
texts.text: Ideally, the chicken should now be left in a refrigerator to [marinate](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Marinating) for at least 24 hours. It may not be safe to leave chicken which has previously been frozen for longer than 24 hours, but fresh chicken should be left to marinate for 48 hours for best results. If you do not wish to leave the chicken to marinate, simply continue to the next step.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: For best results, place on skewers and cook in a [tandoor](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Tandoor) or ceramic pot oven. The chicken can also be cooked on skewers under a medium [grill](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Grilling) for 5-8 minutes on each side or barbecued (cooking times depend on the temperature of the barbecue). Always ensure the chicken is cooked throughout before serving. It should come apart easily when pressed down with the side of a fork.
texts.text: For best results, place on skewers and cook in a [tandoor](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Tandoor) or ceramic pot oven. The chicken can also be cooked on skewers under a medium [grill](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Grilling) for 5-8 minutes on each side or barbecued (cooking times depend on the temperature of the barbecue). Always ensure the chicken is cooked throughout before serving. It should come apart easily when pressed down with the side of a fork.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Serve with [naan](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Naan) , with [rice](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Rice) or use in a [Chicken Tikka Masala](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Chicken_Tikka_Masala) .
texts.text: Serve with [naan](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Naan) , with [rice](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Rice) or use in a [Chicken Tikka Masala](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Chicken_Tikka_Masala) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Mustard seed oil is considered unfit for human consumption in many parts of the world and is therefore not readily available. A poor alternative can be made by crackling 1 [tsp](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Teaspoon) of [mustard](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Mustard) seeds in approximately 2 [tbs](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Tablespoon) of very hot [vegetable](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Vegetable_Oil) or [olive oil](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Olive_Oil) . Allow to cool then use in place of mustard seed oil.
texts.text: Mustard seed oil is considered unfit for human consumption in many parts of the world and is therefore not readily available. A poor alternative can be made by crackling 1 [tsp](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Teaspoon) of [mustard](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Mustard) seeds in approximately 2 [tbs](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Tablespoon) of very hot [vegetable](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Vegetable_Oil) or [olive oil](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Olive_Oil) . Allow to cool then use in place of mustard seed oil.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: It is possible to make a tikka with almost any meat, the most commonly used other than chicken being [lamb](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Lamb) and [mutton](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Mutton) .
texts.text: It is possible to make a tikka with almost any meat, the most commonly used other than chicken being [lamb](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Lamb) and [mutton](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Cookbook:Mutton) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Tikka can be served either as main meal or as an appetiser.
texts.text: Tikka can be served either as main meal or as an appetiser.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Powdered or dried ingredients can be substituted for fresh, but at the expense of taste.
texts.text: Powdered or dried ingredients can be substituted for fresh, but at the expense of taste.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Some people prefer to make tikka with meat on-the-bone for improved flavour.
texts.text: Some people prefer to make tikka with meat on-the-bone for improved flavour.
